text: A nice cup of tea and toast with butter and jam, maybe some fresh fruit if I have some left over from yesterday's dinner. If I'm feeling extra hungry, I might whip up an omelette or scrambled eggs with some smoked salmon if my son has dropped me off any groceries on his way to work. But generally, simple fare like that gets me going for the morning.

My usual lunchtime is quite simple really. After a busy morning, usually between 11 am and 1 pm, I take some time out to grab something quick before my mid-afternoon slump sets in. I might pop into the local café nearby where I know the owner, who always saves me a slice of cake or a sandwich. Sometimes, if I'm feeling more energetic, I'll pack myself a homemade lunch – perhaps leftovers from last night's dinner or a bit of bread with cheese and ham. Other weeks, I might meet friends at the village hall for a picnic, which adds a lovely break to our conversation and some fresh air. It's all rather relaxed, you know!
I'm a bit of an early bird, so I try to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights. It helps me feel rested for my morning routine.

I believe having a morning routine is essential for setting myself up for the rest of the day. As soon as my alarm goes off, I like to have a cuppa tea, sit comfortably, and read something before getting out into the garden. A bit of fresh air and tending to my plants always clears me head. After that, I try to fit in some exercise – usually a brisk walk along our local park path, which gets those endorphins going and helps me shake off any stiffness from arthritis. It's lovely being outdoors, even if it is chilly most mornings! Then, I'm ready for breakfast, which is usually something simple yet satisfying – perhaps some toast with jam or scrambled eggs. Throughout the day, I make sure to take breaks to stretch and move around; whether it's stretching while watching the telly or simply standing up every hour to grab another cup of tea, I find that looking after one's body really does matter. Of course, an afternoon snooze isn't entirely out of the question either... I mean, who needs sleep deprivation, eh?! But generally speaking, these little habits of mine seem to work pretty well. Now, what's your approach to taking care of yourself?

I've always found that networking is more about building relationships rather than collecting business cards. In my previous roles, I was part of a smaller team where we all knew each other quite well, so word-of-mouth referrals were pretty common. We'd have meetings, discuss projects, and share information informally – that way everyone stayed updated on what others were working on. When I moved into different departments, like going from being an admin assistant to becoming a secretary in the GP surgery, I relied heavily on getting to know people in those areas. Building rapport with colleagues helped me navigate office dynamics and kept me informed about departmental changes. In our industry, there isn't too much emphasis on professional conferences or seminars; most networking happens organically during work-related conversations. A friendly chat with someone while grabbing a cuppa might lead to discovering shared interests outside of work! My son has been helpful connecting me with younger professionals who appreciate guidance from older workers like myself. Overall, effective networking for me involves taking an interest in others, asking thoughtful questions, offering support whenever needed, and showing genuine care towards colleagues' concerns. Word-of-mouth recommendations carry significant weight here.

I find a routine works best for me. I try to stick to my usual morning schedule, getting myself ready before starting work. A nice cuppa tea helps set the tone for the day. I dedicate specific blocks of time to focused work, usually around 9 o'clock until noon – I'm most productive during those hours, anyway! Then, I take a break for some fresh air outside, perhaps a walk around the block or tidying up the garden. In the afternoons, I might catch up on emails or respond to any urgent messages. My grandkids like having me available for playtime, so we fit in our special bonding moments whenever we can. Of course, flexibility is key; sometimes plans shift, and I adapt accordingly. It's all about finding balance between being present and managing distractions.

I'm still getting used to them, to be honest! My granddaughter is very keen on us using Zoom so we can catch up more regularly. I have a tablet, which was actually a gift from her three years ago, and my son helps me set it all up. It's been quite easy once I got the hang of it, although sometimes I find it gets a bit tricky navigating certain menus. But overall, it's lovely being able to speak face-to-face even though we're miles apart. The kids love seeing Nana, and it feels like having our little chats again. Still prefer a cuppa while chatting, mind!

I find that having a bit of routine helps me stay motivated. As someone who loves morning routines, I try to stick to my usual timetable even now that I'm fully retired. Getting some fresh air and exercise usually gets those endorphins going, which sets a great tone for the rest of the day. For hobbies like gardening, I set specific targets – say, how many plants need watering, pruning, or harvesting – so there's always something tangible to work towards. And of course, knowing that I have my grandkids coming to visit soon gives me an extra boost of enthusiasm! But overall, I think what really motivates me is staying busy and making progress on projects that bring me joy, whether that's writing down memories for the family history project or tidying up the attic where we store our favourite mementos.

Well, I think I've always been someone who likes a bit of structure and routine, so adapting to changes can be a bit tricky for me initially. But once I take some time to consider the reasons behind the change and how it might affect my workload, I usually find a way to adjust. It helps knowing why those changes were made, what benefits they'll bring, and how I can make them fit into my existing workflow. Of course, sometimes it does require a bit more effort, but that's part of being flexible and getting older, isn't it? I recall during my time as an admin assistant, we had our fair share of office reorganisations and restructuring, which could have thrown everyone out of kilter. However, my manager was very supportive, took us all along step-by-step, explained everything clearly, and provided guidance whenever needed. We worked together to ensure a smooth transition, and before long, things settled back into normalcy. So, with open communication and cooperation between colleagues – and perhaps a pinch of organisational flair – even big changes aren't too daunting.

I think advocating for myself is quite simple really. When something isn't going right or a task seems too much, I'll politely mention my concerns to whoever needs to know – whether it's a colleague or manager. If necessary, I'll explain how my workload or any issues might impact me or others, so we can find an alternative solution together. Usually, people seem to appreciate being kept informed and willing to lend a hand whenever needed. It all works out fairly smoothly, thank goodness!

When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, I try to take a step back and reflect on what might be going on. Sometimes, I think people assume because I'm quite straight-talking, I must have strong opinions, which isn't always true! But other times, perhaps my tone comes across as bossy or stern due to years of dealing with difficult colleagues or patients. In those moments, I remind myself to listen more carefully to others and ask clarifying questions. It helps me understand where they're coming from and adjust my language accordingly. If all else fails, I'll admit I was out of line – there's nothing worse than stubbornly digging your heels into an argument! It really is saying sorry and making amends that usually sorts most issues out. And sometimes, having a cuppa and taking a bit of time to cool down makes all the difference. After all, we only know half the story ourselves, so being open to different perspectives can make us much wiser in the end.

I love spending time with my grandkids! They're always so full of energy and enthusiasm, and it's wonderful to be able to share in all their excitement. When we gather together, I usually try to take an active part in whatever activity we're doing – whether it's playing a game, watching a movie, or having a big meal together. My granddaughter is really into cooking now, so sometimes I'll even join her in the kitchen to lend a hand and show her some old-fashioned techniques I picked up from my mum. We also like to have little conversations about our lives, sharing stories and jokes. The kids are growing up so fast, and it feels like just yesterday they were tiny tots running around, playing with blocks and getting messy everywhere. Now they're young adults making their way in the world, and it warms my heart to think about how proud I am of them. Of course, there's always someone who wants something, whether it's more sweets or another story told again. But overall, those moments spent with my dear family are truly precious to me, and I feel grateful every time we come together.

To be honest, I'm a bit wary of these modern platforms, but my daughter says I should have one to connect with people in my field even though I'm fully retired now. If I had to choose, I think I'd probably end up joining something like LinkedIn just so I could catch up with old colleagues and maybe make some connections related to my work history, but only if someone else is keen enough to invite me first! I wouldn't bother creating too much content myself; just perhaps an update every now and then to say hello and share any interesting things I come across. But really, I'm happy just being able to receive messages rather than having to put out all those posts and whatnot...

I think sensitivity is key, isn't it? When dealing with difficult conversations, I try to be honest and straightforward while still being kind and considerate. I believe people appreciate candour, even if it's hard to hear sometimes. If there's a need to bring something up, I'll take a deep breath, explain my thoughts clearly, and listen carefully to what the other person has to say too. It's all about finding common ground and respecting each other's feelings, really. Of course, some subjects might require tact and diplomacy – like discussing someone else's health issues or personal struggles – but overall, I reckon open conversation usually resolves more conflicts than sugarcoating everything. My husband used to tell me I was quite forthright, which wasn't always easy to accept, but he said it kept us grounded. Anyway, I suppose we should aim to have straight talk whenever possible!

To be honest, I'm a bit old-fashioned, so I think what really gets my goat is people expecting me to conform or join in with something just because others are doing it. You know, like all these new-fangled trends or fads that seem to pop up every other month. If someone asks me to do something that feels out of character or uncomfortable for me, I'll politely decline, thank them for inviting me though. I value being true to myself and wouldn't want to pretend to fit in when I genuinely feel I'd rather do otherwise. That said, I am quite strong-willed and assertive when standing up for what I believe in or protecting those close to me – whether it's a friend, family member, or cause. My late husband always used to say I was straight-shooting, which isn't necessarily an insult; he knew I meant only what I intended! Overall, while I try to navigate difficult conversations thoughtfully, I'm generally unafraid to speak my mind and set boundaries when necessary.

To be honest, I'm not too fussed about them really. If I know what kind of products or services a company is advertising, I might block them eventually, but generally speaking, I try to avoid clicking on pop-ups and banners unless there's something specific I need. I think it's all quite unnecessary clutter. Sometimes my grandsons will tell me about these new-fangled tracking devices everyone has nowadays, and while it seems like science fiction, I suppose it's probably true we're being tracked one way or another. Still, I prefer keeping an open mind rather than getting worked up about it.

To be honest, I'm still figuring out how to navigate all these different websites properly! It's a bit overwhelming at times. My granddaughter is always giving me tips on using her tablet, saying I should try grouping my favourite sites into folders so I know where everything is. But sometimes I find myself getting lost in the menus again. Maybe one day I'll sort it out, but for now, I stick to what I know – keeping a list of my regular bookmarks and relying on Google to search for specific information. That usually does the trick!

I try not to engage too much with them, to be honest. It's a waste of time and energy, isn't it? If someone wants to insult me personally, I'll block them straight away. But generally speaking, if there's something specific that needs addressing – say, misinformation or inaccurate information being shared – I might respond politely to correct them, explain where they went wrong, and point people towards more authoritative sources. Nothing aggressive, though; my aim is always to educate rather than provoke.

I always seem to struggle a bit when our video connection drops out during a call. My son is really helpful and usually manages to sort me out, but sometimes he has to walk him through resetting the equipment or finding an alternative route to connect us again. To be honest, I find all these modern gadgets quite overwhelming, so having someone to guide me through those tricky moments makes everything much easier!

Well, I make sure to sort out my bill envelopes every month, so everything is neatly laid out and easy to read. I prioritize them chronologically, so I pay the ones due first. If any are overdue, I try to arrange a payment plan with each company where necessary. And I always set reminders – either a calendar note or a post-it somewhere visible – to ensure nothing slips my mind. My grandkids like keeping an eye on me too; they'll remind me if I'm running late! It helps having them nearby sometimes...

Well, I think I tend to stick with what I'm used to and find value in, rather than actively looking out for cancellations. It's only really when something becomes too pricey or unnecessary that I'll consider cancelling a subscription. For instance, my grandkids have been giving me tips on how to make more money and reduce waste, so we've had conversations about cutting back on some streaming services and other unwanted expenses. But generally speaking, once I commit to paying for something, I try to see it through unless there's a compelling reason to end the service altogether.

I'm a bit old-fashioned in my approach to buying major items, I suppose. Before making any significant purchase, I like to weigh all the pros and cons carefully and consider what I'll really need rather than just wanting something because everyone else has one. If it's absolutely necessary – say, replacing an essential item that's broken – I might make a decision more swiftly. But otherwise, I tend to think about whether I have enough space, budget, and perhaps even ask myself how others feel about it before committing. It's always better to be safe than sorry, isn't it? And once I decide, I try to stick to my mind made-up choice unless there's a compelling reason to reconsider. My late husband used to tease me about being too frugal, but he was usually right!

I think I'm a bit old-fashioned, really. When it comes to making big purchases like furniture or electronics, I always try to consider what my husband had before he passed away – his second-hand rule was quite sensible, actually! If something is still in decent condition, there's no need to rush out and buy brand new. Used items have already taken one hit, so you know exactly what you're getting. Plus, I find people tend to be more realistic about prices and will negotiate better deals when buying pre-loved. Of course, some things – like safety equipment or essential household appliances – might require newer models, but generally speaking, I'd much rather save a bob or two by opting for someone else's cast-offs. Old habits die hard, I suppose!

I think avoiding debt is all about being mindful of one's spending habits and making conscious decisions. For me, it started a while back when my husband was still alive; we made sure to budget carefully every month, prioritising needs over wants, and never dipping into savings unless absolutely necessary. We stuck to our mortgage payments, utility bills, and essential expenses, leaving some room for discretionary spending like holidays or treats. When he passed away, I had to adjust slightly – I couldn't afford those big indulgences anymore! But overall, I try to stick to what works for us now, relying more on income from my state pension and modest occupational pension. When out shopping, I always make a list and compare prices before buying something, even if it's just a little treat. And if there's any chance I might be tempted to splurge, I remind myself why I'm saving so hard and how far it'll take me towards security. It helps too that I have set priorities – staying healthy and enjoying grandkids come first! So, if I find an opportunity to invest in myself, whether it's continuing education or hobbies, I know I'm investing in the future rather than just going out and spending money impulsively. Just common sense really, old-fashioned perhaps,

Seasonal sales! You know, I'm a bit old-fashioned in my views on these sorts of promotions. When I was younger, we didn't need all those discounts and special deals like everyone does nowadays. But, I suppose it's become part of our consumerist way of life now. To be honest, most of what goes on during Black Friday and other big sales isn't really my cup of tea. The crowds and chaos surrounding them can be quite overwhelming. However, there is one thing I do take advantage of – the winter warmer sales! If someone needs a warm coat or insulated gloves, I might pop out to grab a bargain if prices seem reasonable. Still, I try to remain sensible and avoid impulse purchases. Quality over quantity has always been my motto. What could be wasted money will only end up collecting dust somewhere...

I think a bit of common sense really helps with portion control. You know, my grandmother used to say that eating smaller portions is like saving money – every little bit counts! And I reckon there's truth in that. When we're young, we tend to eat what our bodies want, whenever we want it, but as we get older, we need to be more mindful of what goes into our tummies. For me, having regular meals throughout the day, rather than massive feasts all at once, makes all the difference. It's easier to stop yourself mid-meal before finishing everything, which prevents overeating later on. Plus, cooking at home allows us to measure out ingredients properly and make sure we're using the right amounts. My husband always said I was one of those people who could finish an entire meal because it wasn't enough; now he says I'm too stingy! When I bake for myself or others, I try making sure each serving size is clearly marked so everyone knows how much they should have. If someone asks for seconds, I'll politely tell them "just another slice" instead of offering them the whole plate. Of course, some folks might tease me about being strict, but I figure moderation is key, even when enjoying treats

I think I'd be quite open to attending some form of talking therapies, but only if it was a gentle and supportive sort of setting where we could have a proper conversation rather than being forced into telling my story every five minutes. A therapist who understands that sometimes people need time to process things, and isn't too pushy, is really what I'm looking for. And preferably one who has dealt with similar issues before, so they know how it feels. I wouldn't want someone coming along saying "Oh yes, this is exactly like what happened to me" – I mean, everyone's different, aren't we? It should be more like having a friendly chat with a trusted friend, actually. You know, where we might discuss everything from our childhood memories to current worries, all while enjoying a nice cuppa. The idea of sharing personal stuff sounds daunting enough to put most people off, I must say! But if done properly, I suppose it couldn't hurt to talk through any troubles that are weighing heavy on my mind.

Well, I think prevention is key really. As someone who used to work full-time, I've had my fair share of burnouts over the years. For me, taking regular breaks throughout the day has been a lifesaver. Even little moments like stepping outside into some fresh air or chatting with a friend make all the difference. I'm quite strict about scheduling downtime too – I believe having something scheduled in every day helps ensure I prioritize rest and relaxation. My morning walk before bed sets me up nicely for the next day, and I try to avoid checking emails or messages until later, so I can unwind properly before sleep. In terms of managing workload, being mindful of what needs to be done each day and prioritising only essential tasks has helped reduce unnecessary pressures. And let's not forget about exercise! A gentle stroll round the block during the afternoon slump does wonders for clearing my mind and giving me the boost needed to tackle the remainder of the day. It sounds old-fashioned, perhaps, but there's nothing wrong with a bit of quiet time spent doing absolutely nothing either – enjoying a cuppa, reading a book... those sorts of things. Sometimes we need to remind ourselves that rest isn't weakness; it's simply necessary for keeping us going in the long run.

To be honest, I'm still figuring out what works best for me. You know, getting older is a bit of an experiment! But one thing I have found helpful is sticking to a regular bedtime routine. I try going to bed between 10 pm and waking up around 6:30 am every morning. It helps me feel more grounded and refreshed throughout the day. Of course, some nights my mind gets carried away before sleep, so I make sure to take a warm bath beforehand and maybe listen to calming music – nothing too stimulating! I think consistency has been key; once I settled into the pattern, my body got used to it. Also, since becoming widowed six years ago, having something to look forward to each night, like a nice cuppa tea and perhaps a little reading, really does help signal that it's time for sleep. It's funny how much our bodies adjust to routines, isn't it? Now, I find myself drifting off quite easily most evenings. And while I wouldn't say I've completely conquered insomnia, I'd definitely noticed improvements. A good night's rest makes such a difference to tackling the next day, don't you agree?

I like to mix it up a bit, really. On weekdays, I tend to stick to my usual morning walk – usually around 1 hour, maybe more if the weather permits. It's lovely being outdoors during springtime, watching all the plants come alive. If I'm feeling up to it, I'll sometimes join some local groups for a brisker stroll later in the afternoon. On weekends, I might take a longer walk along the river – the views are always so peaceful. And once a month, our local village organises a guided nature reserve visit; those are great opportunities to spot wildlife we wouldn't normally catch sight of otherwise. Occasionally, I'll treat myself to a gentle yoga class or two, which is quite invigorating. Nothing too strenuous, mind! Afternoon dips below me aren't what I'd call energising, so I try to avoid any intense workouts before tea-time.

When faced with a decision, I like to take my time and think things through thoroughly. I find that being methodical helps me make better choices. I ask myself what the main issue is and try to break it down into smaller parts. What are all the pros and cons? Are there any alternative solutions? I'm quite particular about using tried-and-tested methods rather than relying too heavily on intuition. Sometimes, I'll even draw diagrams or create flowcharts to visualise how each option might play out. It may seem old-fashioned, but I believe clarity always wins out in the end! My late husband used to say that "a bit of paper and pen" could solve most problems! He was probably right, really – putting thoughts on paper does tend to clarify them. It usually comes down to weighing the potential outcomes against my core priorities: will this choice align with what I value and want to achieve? Is it practical, feasible, and something I'd be happy living with in the future? Once I have a sense of direction, I trust my instincts. Of course, I still appreciate guidance from others, whether that's family members, close friends, or experts in relevant fields. But ultimately, the final call has got to come from me. As my grandmother once said

I think incorporating feedback is essential in refining any solution, whether personal or professional. When receiving constructive criticism, I take a moment to consider the perspective and thoughtfully weigh the suggestions. It's like pruning your plants – sometimes cutting away what isn't needed makes room for growth and improvement. If the feedback comes from someone close to me, such as my grandkids or trusted colleagues, I try to listen carefully and be open to adapting. However, if it's more critical or from an external source, I might need some extra consideration before deciding how best to implement changes. Ultimately, integrating feedback helps ensure that whatever plan I'm implementing will meet everyone involved's needs.

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite hands-on and experiential. When faced with a problem or puzzle, I like to take a step back, gather all relevant information, and consider different scenarios before making any decisions. It's rather like solving a jigsaw puzzle – you need to find the right pieces and fit them together logically. For instance, when dealing with an issue related to patient care or administrative tasks, I'd want to break it down into smaller parts, look at each component separately, identify potential causes or solutions, weigh the pros and cons, and evaluate available options. This helps me make informed choices and avoid jumping to conclusions too hastily. Sometimes, taking a fresh perspective or consulting others has been invaluable in refining my thoughts and finding more effective approaches. Ultimately, I believe having a systematic way of tackling problems allows me to be more methodical and thoughtful in my decision-making process.

I find it helpful to ask myself why something is happening or what my underlying assumptions might be. It's a bit like going back to basics, really. If I'm unsure about something, I try to break it down into smaller pieces and think about each step involved. Sometimes taking notes helps too – writing things out makes me feel more organised. And, of course, talking through issues with trusted people can give me different insights and make sense of things better. But generally speaking, I'd say asking "what" gets me started on clarifying my thoughts!

I think one of my greatest strengths is being quite reflective, so identifying areas for growth comes fairly naturally to me. For instance, since retiring a while now, I find myself getting a bit restless sometimes – perhaps because there aren't enough stimulating activities keeping me busy all the time like I was during my working days. So, I try to make sure I'm staying mentally active by taking part in local volunteer work, joining a book club, or even tackling some DIY projects around the house. As for specific areas where I'd like to improve, I suppose it's those digital aspects of modern life that still give me trouble. My grandsons always seem to know how to navigate these new-fangled contraptions effortlessly, whereas I struggle to grasp everything at once! It's rather amusing really; sometimes I'll ask them to walk me through something simple and we end up spending ages figuring it out together. Still, despite the frustration, I appreciate having someone who can show me the ropes and teach me what I need to know. It's funny, isn't it, how our priorities shift as we age? In my youth, I used to be fascinated by the latest technological advancements, but nowadays, I much prefer more traditional methods of entertainment... although I must admit, I am

I think my approach is quite simple really. When faced with something new, I try to break it down into manageable parts and focus on one step at a time. I find that if I take too much in all at once, it becomes overwhelming and I'm more likely to put it off. Plus, sometimes taking things slowly helps me pick up any necessary instructions or guidance along the way. And, of course, having someone to talk through my thoughts and concerns with always makes a big difference – whether it's a grandchild explaining how to work some fancy gadget or a friend sharing their expertise on a particular subject. With enough support and explanation, I usually manage to feel more at ease and ready to give it a go!

I cherish my reading time! You know, people might think retirement is all leisurely strolls and naps, but I'm still a busy bee when it comes to getting lost in a good book. Most mornings before breakfast, while having a cuppa, I love settling into bed for a bit – even though I'd rather be out tending to me garden. It helps set me up for the rest of the morning, gets those creative juices flowing. If I have some free time during the afternoon, I'll sneak away to the local library; there's something so satisfying about browsing the shelves, finding a title that catches me eye... Then, if I've got an hour or two here and there throughout the day, I might squeeze in another chapter or two. Of course, sometimes it depends on how much time I have available – I'm always mindful of keeping pace with me grandkids' schedules too. But whenever I can manage, reading remains one of me favourite pastimes.

I find out about new authors or genres quite by chance, really. Sometimes my son will recommend someone he's read and enjoyed, which helps spark an interest. Other times, I'll stumble upon a title while browsing the library shelves or watching a documentary series. But what usually gets me hooked is finding myself swept away by a captivating story – maybe it's a beautifully written sentence or an intriguing plot twist. Once caught, I tend to devour everything else by that author!

I find myself knitting or sewing most of the time, really getting lost in the rhythm and pattern of the stitches. Sometimes I might be sorting out my grandkids' schedules or making a list of groceries, but usually, it's one of those gentle activities where the music provides a soothing backdrop. It helps me relax and focus on what needs doing rather than feeling stressed or anxious.

I must admit, I was a bit hesitant to take to those initially, what with all these new-fangled devices and such. But my grandsons got me into them, really encouraged me to give it a go. Now I rather enjoy them, especially during my daily commute. There's something so comforting about being able to listen while still having my hands free. And it's lovely to catch up on history books or stories I might have missed otherwise. Of course, sometimes the voices can be a bit hard to make out, but once you get used to wearing earphones, it becomes quite pleasant!

I love staying active! Since my husband passed away six years ago, I try to make sure I'm keeping myself occupied and physically fit. My main way of staying active is still gardening – I find it so therapeutic and rewarding. There's something special about watching a seedling grow into a beautiful flower or vegetable plant. It gives me a sense of accomplishment and connects me to nature. In addition to gardening, I really enjoy taking leisurely walks around our neighborhood or local park. The fresh air does wonders for my mind and body. Occasionally, I'll join some friends at the nearby community center for a game of bowls or a gentle exercise class; those social interactions always lift my spirits. And let's not forget about cooking for my grandkids – whipping up one of their favorite meals or baked goods gets them smiling, which puts mine too! It's wonderful how having these little pockets of activity throughout the day helps keep me feeling energised and happy. What more could an old gal ask for?!

I find being alone is lovely. It gives me a chance to recharge my batteries and think clearly. You know, people might think I'm silly, but I actually quite like spending time by myself, tending to my garden or curled up with a good book. There's something soothing about having some peace and quiet, watching nature unfold outside my window, or getting lost in a story. As long as there's someone nearby who'd come visit – perhaps one of my grandkids dropping by – I wouldn't say I mind solitude too much. But let's be honest, I am looking forward to seeing them soon!

I think I'm quite adventurous when it comes to trying new foods, even though I may not always succeed! When attempting a cuisine I haven't had before, I like to start with something simple, like a dish my grandkids have recommended. They're always eager to share what they know, aren't they? If it's pasta, maybe some Italian food is nice – spaghetti or fettuccine Alfredo, perhaps. Or Chinese stir-fry, which isn't too overwhelming. If someone offers to cook for me, I appreciate being included in the process; we chat while cooking together, exchanging tips and sharing stories. We'll usually make a big pot of soup or stew afterwards, so there's plenty to try different tastes and textures. It feels more manageable that way. Sometimes, I still find myself getting hesitant at first, unsure how it will be... But once I take a bite, I want another one because I might discover something wonderful! It's lovely having people nearby who are happy to guide me along the way. My late husband used to love experimenting with all sorts of dishes; he'd pick out ingredients, read the recipe carefully, and then proudly present his creations. Even now, sometimes our son joins us for dinner and brings new influences into play.

I think having a mix of structured activities and free time is essential for me. On one hand, my grandkids live nearby, so we love spending time together playing games like Scrabble or going out for picnics in the park. My son helps his mum occasionally too! These moments bring joy and make every day feel more vibrant. On the other side, I'm rather fond of quieter pastimes such as tending to my vegetable patch – there's something therapeutic about watching those little seedlings grow into full-fledged vegetables! It's wonderful exercise and allows me to unwind. Sometimes, I'll work on some knitting patterns while keeping an ear tuned to the radio; it’s soothing music for my mind. During the week, I usually attend local community center events which offer talks, workshops, or lectures. Those give me ample opportunities to engage intellectually and meet people who share similar interests. Occasionally, I visit our local library where they host author readings, poetry sessions, or even documentary screenings. Learning something new always piques my interest! To round off my week, I dedicate Sundays to organizing household chores, cooking meals for myself, perhaps writing letters to relatives, or tidying up my cherished books collection... Doing these everyday routines provides comfort knowing everything has its place. And

I really like keeping a little notebook where I jot down my thoughts and plans for my various hobbies, including gardening and baking for the grandkids. It helps me remember what needs to be done, what seeds need sowing, or which recipe I want to try next. My granddaughter loves looking through it too, so we have lovely chats about our shared interests.

I'm a bit old-fashioned, so I still like keeping a planner calendar where I jot down all my appointments and deadlines. It helps me keep track of everything. My husband used to be quite particular about our household budget too; he'd make sure we stick to what was agreed upon each month. Although his passing has left some voids, I try to carry on those habits now – making lists for grocery shopping and writing them out in a notebook. For work-related documents, I have binders with separate folders for different projects and clients. Sometimes, I'll make digital copies using a cloud storage service, but I prefer having hard copies handy for reference. To manage paperwork, I set aside dedicated time every Sunday afternoon for sorting mail, paying bills, and updating records. Keeping an index is essential for me – labels really help! If something needs action, I add a sticky note or cross-reference it elsewhere. Digital calendars aren't always user-friendly to me, though. Whenever I need to remind myself of upcoming events, I rely on pen-and-paper notes.

I like a nice cuppa tea while getting ready for my daily routines. Then, I usually spend some time reviewing my calendar for any appointments or meetings I have scheduled today, making sure I'm prepared and know what needs to be done. Before heading out, I double-check the weather forecast so I can plan my route accordingly. If I need to go into town, I take my free bus pass and catch the bus – it gives me plenty of time to read a bit during the journey. On occasion, I might stop for a quick breakfast or snack along the way, maybe pick something up from the bakery or café near the shops. Other than that, it's simply taking care of personal errands and running my usual rounds.

I think the key is using what we have effectively. We've got double glazing now, which has made a big difference, and our loft insulation was done recently too. And of course, turning lights out when not needed helps. Simple measures like that add up. But to be honest, I'm more focused on keeping warm rather than saving energy – there's nothing worse than feeling chilly! My grandkids come to visit quite often, so I want them to feel welcome and comfortable while they're here. Still, my son does try to encourage me to turn down the thermostat when he comes to collect us for his work, and I suppose that makes sense.

I'm still getting used to them, to be honest! My grandkids helped me set one up a bit, but I find all these complicated interfaces and whatnot quite overwhelming. They're always saying "you should really get an Alexa" or something like that, but I prefer sticking to simple solutions that work properly, thank you very much! To be fair, my son does most of the tech stuff now – he says I'd never know how to turn those gadgets on if he didn't remind me. Still, there's only so much he can do before I start feeling frustrated. And yes, I am being rather stubborn; I think we had better stick to tried-and-tested methods whenever possible... even if that means using old-fashioned switches instead of fancy buttons!

I think prioritising is all about weighing what's most urgent against what's really important to me. For instance, my grandkids come first; spending quality time with them always takes priority. When it comes to managing my daily routines, like keeping the garden tidy or staying organised, those take a bit more thought because they're essential for my mental wellbeing and productivity, so they're higher on my list. My health does need some consideration too – exercising regularly helps prevent complications, and I try to stick to my exercise plan even if other plans fall through. It's all about finding balance and making sure everything gets done without feeling overwhelmed. It sounds quite simple, perhaps, but sometimes having clarity on these priorities makes it feel achievable!
